chunk: Linear filtering: Separable filtering, Band -pass and steerable filters Why Do We Need Filters in Image Processing? Images contain: Useful information Ôćĺ edges, textures, shapes, objects ´éĚ Undesired components Ôćĺ noise, blur, illumination variations ´éĚ Filters help us: Enhance important features ´éĚ Suppress irrelevant information ´éĚ Prepare images for higher-level tasks (segmentation, recognition, ´éĚ tracking) Linear filters are preferred because they are: Mathematically tractable ´éĚ Computationally efficient ´éĚ Easy to analyze in both spatial and frequency domains ´éĚ 1. Separable Filters What Is a Separable Filter? A filter is separable if a 2D convolution can be broken into two 1D convolutions: Instead of filtering the image in 2D directly, we: 1. Filter along rows 2. Filter along columns Why Are Separable Filters Used? 1. Computational Efficiency 2D convolution with kernel Ôćĺ operations per pixel ´éĚ Separable filter Ôćĺ operations per pixel ´éĚ This is critical for real-time systems ´éĚ

2. Memory and Hardware Efficiency Fewer multiplications and additions ´éĚ Ideal for embedded systems, GPUs, and mobile vision applications ´éĚ 3. Mathematical Simplicity Many important filters (Gaussian, Sobel) are naturally separable ´éĚ Enables efficient multi-scale processing ´éĚ Common Examples Gaussian smoothing filter ´éĚ Sobel edge detector ´éĚ Prewitt filter ´éĚ Advantages Very fast computation ´éĚ Reduced computational complexity ´éĚ Easy to implement ´éĚ Suitable for large kernel sizes ´éĚ Limitations Not all filters are separable ´éĚ Limited ability to model complex 2D patterns ´éĚ Orientation selectivity is weak unless combined with other filters ´éĚ Applications Image smoothing and denoising ´éĚ Gradient and edge computation ´éĚ Preprocessing in CNNs ´éĚ Medical image smoothing ´éĚ Video processing pipelines ´éĚ

2. Band-Pass Filters What Is a Band-Pass Filter? A band-pass filter allows only a specific range of frequencies to pass while blocking: Low frequencies Ôćĺ illumination, background ´éĚ High frequencies Ôćĺ noise ´éĚ It isolates mid-frequency information, which often corresponds to: Edges ´éĚ Textures ´éĚ Repetitive patterns ´éĚ Why Are Band-Pass Filters Used? 1. Feature Enhancement Most meaningful visual structures lie in specific frequency bands ´éĚ Band-pass filtering enhances these structures ´éĚ 2. Noise and Illumination Suppression Low-frequency lighting variations are removed ´éĚ High-frequency noise is suppressed ´éĚ 3. Scale Selectivity Objects appear at different scales ´éĚ Band-pass filters can be tuned to specific sizes ´éĚ Common Band-Pass Filters Difference of Gaussians (DoG) Subtracts two blurred images ´éĚ Approximates Laplacian of Gaussian ´éĚ Efficient and widely used ´éĚ Laplacian of Gaussian (LoG) Detects zero crossings corresponding to edges ´éĚ Strong theoretical foundation ´éĚ

Gabor Filters Jointly selective to frequency and orientation ´éĚ Inspired by human visual cortex ´éĚ Advantages Excellent for texture and edge analysis ´éĚ Multi-scale analysis possible ´éĚ Good balance between noise suppression and detail preservation ´éĚ Limitations Parameter selection (scale, frequency) is critical ´éĚ Sensitive to scale mismatch ´éĚ Computationally more expensive than simple smoothing ´éĚ Applications Edge detection ´éĚ Texture classification ´éĚ Face and fingerprint recognition ´éĚ Medical imaging (tumor boundary detection) ´éĚ Remote sensing and satellite imagery ´éĚ Feature extraction in machine learning ´éĚ 3. Steerable Filters What Is a Steerable Filter? A steerable filter can be oriented to any angle by combining a small set of basis filters: No physical rotation of the filter is required. Why Are Steerable Filters Used? 1. Orientation Selectivity Real-world images contain edges at arbitrary orientations ´éĚ Fixed-direction filters miss many features ´éĚ

2. Computational Efficiency Instead of rotating filters many times, responses are synthesized ´éĚ mathematically Reduces redundancy and computation ´éĚ 3. Robust Feature Representation Provides continuous orientation information ´éĚ Improves rotation invariance ´éĚ Advantages Precise orientation tuning ´éĚ No interpolation artifacts from rotation ´éĚ Fewer filters needed ´éĚ Ideal for multi-orientation analysis ´éĚ Limitations Requires pre-defined basis filters ´éĚ More complex mathematical formulation ´éĚ Higher memory overhead for storing basis responses ´éĚ Applications Edge and contour detection ´éĚ Corner and junction detection ´éĚ Texture segmentation ´éĚ Object and shape recognition ´éĚ Medical image analysis (vessel detection) ´éĚ Autonomous driving (lane and boundary detection) ´éĚ Comparison: Aspect Separable Band-Pass Steerable Filters

Neighborhood operators What Are Neighborhood Operators? A neighborhood operator computes the output value of a pixel using the values of its neighboring pixels. Unlike point operators (which use only one pixel), neighborhood operators consider local spatial context. Mathematical Definition For an image , a neighborhood operator produces: Where: Ôćĺ neighborhood around pixel ´éĚ Ôćĺ operation (linear or non-linear) ´éĚ Types of Neighborhoods (a) 4-Neighborhood (x,yÔłĺ1) (xÔłĺ1,y) (x,y) (x+1,y) (x,y+1) Used for: Simple connectivity ´éĚ Low-complexity operations ´éĚ (b) 8-Neighborhood (xÔłĺ1,yÔłĺ1) (x,yÔłĺ1) (x+1,yÔłĺ1) (xÔłĺ1,y) (x,y) (x+1,y) (xÔłĺ1,y+1) (x,y+1) (x+1,y+1)

Used for: Smoothing ´éĚ Edge detection ´éĚ Texture analysis ´éĚ 1. Non-Linear Filtering What Is Non-Linear Filtering? A non-linear filter is a neighborhood operator where the output is not a linear weighted sum of pixel values. Here, is a non-linear function such as: Median ´éĚ Minimum ´éĚ Maximum ´éĚ Rank selection ´éĚ Why Non-Linear Filtering Is Used Linear filters: Smooth noise ´éĚ But blur edges and fine details ´éĚ Non-linear filters: Remove noise without averaging across edges ´éĚ Preserve important structures ´éĚ Common Non-Linear Filters

1. Median Filter (Most Important) Operation Replace the center pixel with the median of neighborhood ´éĚ values Example (3├Ś3 Neighborhood) Example (3├Ś3 Neighborhood) Sorted values: Median = 14 Advantages Excellent for salt-and-pepper noise Preserves edges Simple to implement Limitations Not effective for Gaussian noise Computationally expensive for large windows Applications Medical image denoising ´éĚ Removal of impulse noise ´éĚ Preprocessing for segmentation ´éĚ 2. Bilateral Filtering What Is Bilateral Filtering?

Bilateral filtering is a non-linear, edge-preserving smoothing filter that considers: 1. Spatial closeness 2. Intensity similarity Unlike Gaussian filtering, it does not blur edges. Mathematical Expression Where: Ôćĺ spatial spread ´éĚ Ôćĺ intensity similarity ´éĚ Ôćĺ normalization factor ´éĚ Why Bilateral Filtering Is Used Gaussian filter blurs edges ´éĚ Bilateral filter smooths only similar pixels ´éĚ Preserves object boundaries ´éĚ Intuition ÔÇťAverage only nearby pixels that look similar.ÔÇŁ Advantages Ôťö Preserves edges Ôťö Reduces noise effectively Ôťö Suitable for real-world images Limitations Computationally expensive Parameter tuning is critical

Not suitable for real-time without optimization Applications Image denoising ´éĚ HDR imaging ´éĚ Depth map smoothing ´éĚ Medical image enhancement ´éĚ Artistic image abstraction ´éĚ 3. Binary Image Processing What Is Binary Image Processing? Binary image processing deals with images having only two pixel values: 0 Ôćĺ background ´éĚ 1 Ôćĺ object ´éĚ Binary images are usually obtained after thresholding. Why Binary Image Processing Is Used Simplifies image analysis ´éĚ Focuses on shape and structure ´éĚ Reduces computational complexity ´éĚ Basic Binary Neighborhood Operations 1. Logical Operations AND ´éĚ OR ´éĚ NOT ´éĚ XOR ´éĚ 2. Morphological Operations (Most Important)

These are non-linear neighborhood operators using a structuring element. (a) Erosion Removes boundary pixels Ôťö Shrinks objects Ôťö Removes noise (b) Dilation Adds pixels to boundaries Ôťö Fills gaps Ôťö Connects components (c) Opening (Erosion Ôćĺ Dilation) Ôťö Removes small objects Ôťö Smooths contours (d) Closing (Dilation Ôćĺ Erosion) Ôťö Fills holes Ôťö Bridges narrow gaps Applications of Binary Image Processing Object detection ´éĚ OCR and document analysis ´éĚ Medical image segmentation ´éĚ Industrial inspection ´éĚ Fingerprint recognition ´éĚ

Geometric Transformations A geometric transformation changes the position, orientation, size, or shape of an object in an image or signal without changing the pixel values themselves. Why Geometric Transformations are Needed ´éĚ Image alignment (registration) Rotation and scaling of images ´éĚ Object tracking ´éĚ ´éĚ Medical image analysis ´éĚ Satellite and aerial image correction Computer graphics and animation ´éĚ Basic Idea (Pixel Mapping) A transformation maps: Two ways to map: 1. Forward mapping ÔÇô move pixels forward 2. Backward mapping ÔÇô find source pixel for each output pixel (preferred) Types of Geometric Transformations A. Rigid Transformations ´éĚ Translation ´éĚ Rotation ´éĚ Preserve shape and size B. Similarity Transformations ´éĚ Translation ´éĚ Rotation ´éĚ Uniform scaling C. Affine Transformations ´éĚ Translation ´éĚ Rotation

´éĚ Scaling ´éĚ Shearing ´éĚ Preserve parallel lines D. Projective Transformations ´éĚ Perspective effects ´éĚ Do NOT preserve parallelism Mathematical Representation (Homogeneous Coordinates) To combine transformations easily, we use homogeneous coordinates: 1. Translation Meaning Move an object left/right/up/down Equation Matrix form Numerical Example 1. Point: Translate by: Ôťů New point = (6, 5) 2. Scaling Meaning Change size of object Equation Matrix

Point: Scale factors: Ôťů New point = (6, 2) 3. Rotation Meaning Rotate around origin (counter-clockwise) Equation Matrix Point: Rotate by 90┬░ Ôťů New point = (0, 1) Classification of Geometric Transformations Geometric transformations can be broadly classified into: 1. Parametric (Global) Transformations 2. Non-parametric (Local) Transformations o Mesh-based warping o Spline-based warping This classification depends on whether the transformation is governed by a small fixed set of parameters or by many local control points. Parametric Transformations A geometric transformation in which the mapping between input and output coordinates is defined by a finite set of parameters that globally control image deformation.

Concept A parametric transformation is a geometric transformation in which the mapping between input and output coordinates is defined using a finite number of parameters. These parameters globally control the deformation of the entire image. All pixels undergo the same transformation rule. Mathematical Representation General parametric form: where: ´éĚ = original coordinates ´éĚ = transformed coordinates ´éĚ = transformation parameters Common Parametric Transformations (a) Translation (2 parameters) What are the parameters? ´éĚ Ôćĺ horizontal shift ´éĚ Ôćĺ vertical shift These two values completely define the translation. (b) Scaling (2 parameters) What are the parameters? ´éĚ Ôćĺ scaling along x-direction ´éĚ Ôćĺ scaling along y-direction These two numbers define how much stretching/shrinking happens. (c) Rotation (1 parameter) What is the parameter? ´éĚ Ôćĺ rotation angle Just one number decides everything.

(d) Affine Transformation (6 parameters) Parameters: Paramete Meaning r (a) scaling / rotation (b) shear / rotation (c) shear / rotation (d) scaling / rotation (t_x) translation in x (t_y) translation in y Affine transformations preserve: ´éĚ Straight lines ´éĚ Parallelism but not angles or lengths. Homogeneous Coordinate Representation To combine multiple parametric transformations, homogeneous coordinates are used: Properties of Parametric Transformations Ôťö Global transformation Ôťö Few parameters Ôťö Computationally efficient Ôťö Easy to estimate and invert Applications ´éĚ Image registration ´éĚ Camera motion correction ´éĚ Medical image alignment ´éĚ Object tracking ´éĚ Document image correction

Mesh-Based Warping A non-parametric image warping technique that divides the image into a mesh and applies local transformations using displaced control points. Concept Mesh-based warping is a local, non-parametric geometric transformation in which the image is divided into a grid (mesh). Each mesh vertex acts as a control point. By moving these control points, local deformations are created. Different regions of the image can deform differently. Mesh Construction 1. Divide the image into: o Rectangular mesh, or o Triangular mesh 2. Define control points at mesh vertices 3. Specify new positions for control points Warping Process (Step-by-Step) 1. Mesh generation over original image 2. Control point displacement 3. Local transformation of each mesh cell 4. Interpolation of pixels inside each cell Mathematical Formulation (Conceptual) Let: ´éĚ = original mesh vertex ´éĚ = displaced mesh vertex

For a point inside a mesh element: where: ´éĚ are interpolation weights ´éĚ for rectangular mesh ´éĚ for triangular mesh Interpolation Methods ´éĚ Bilinear interpolation (rectangular mesh) ´éĚ Barycentric interpolation (triangular mesh) Properties of Mesh-Based Warping Ôťö Local deformation capability Ôťö Highly flexible Ôťö Suitable for non-rigid transformations ÔŁî Higher computational cost ÔŁî Requires many control points ÔŁî More complex implementation Applications ´éĚ Face morphing and animation ´éĚ Medical image deformation modeling ´éĚ Image editing and special effects ´éĚ Shape matching ´éĚ Non-rigid image registration Example 1: Point: (x,y)=(2,3) perform Translation. Formula Parameters Step-by-step Output point
